news_summary: Today's space news highlights inspiring achievements in lunar exploration and ongoing successful Mars missions showcasing innovation and perseverance.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité spatiale d'aujourd'hui met en avant des réalisations inspirantes dans le domaine de l'exploration lunaire ainsi que les missions sur Mars en cours, qui témoignent d'innovation et de persévérance.
news_summary_es: Las noticias espaciales de hoy destacan logros inspiradores en la exploración lunar y las exitosas misiones a Marte que se están llevando a cabo, y que ponen de manifiesto la innovación y la perseverancia.

articles.title: I Am Artemis: Kathleen Harmon
articles.summary: Listen to this audio excerpt from Kathleen Harmon, the Artemis II Mission Interface Manager for NASA’s Deep Space Network: Captivated by Apollo launches on her television as a child, Kathleen Harmon now plays a key role in NASA’s Artemis program. Harmon serves as the Artemis II mission interface manager for NASA’s Deep Space Network, an […]
articles.content:
Listen to this audio excerpt from Kathleen Harmon, the Artemis II Mission Interface Manager for NASA’s Deep Space Network:













0:00 / 0:00 Your browser does not support the audio element.

Captivated by Apollo launches on her television as a child, Kathleen Harmon now plays a key role in NASA’s Artemis program.

Harmon serves as the Artemis II mission interface manager for NASA’s Deep Space Network, an international array of giant radio antennas which are used to communicate with spacecraft. Managed by the agency’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California, the Deep Space Network is the largest scientific telecommunications system in the world, supporting more than 40 missions exploring deep space. The network is also a key component of NASA’s Moon-bound Artemis missions.

Kathleen Harmon, Artemis II Mission Interface Manager for NASA’s Deep Space Network, in the Charles Elachi Mission Control Center at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California. NASA/JPL-Caltech

“If you’re in a car and you’re going somewhere and you don’t have GPS or a cellphone, you might get lost, or you might not be able to tell someone that you’re lost,” said Harmon, illustrating how the Deep Space Network “talks” to spacecraft. “The network provides that lifeline to spacecraft across the solar system, and even interstellar space, so that they can talk to Earth and send back amazing science data, images, and videos from Mars rovers, space telescopes, orbiters, and more.”

In her role as a mission interface manager, and with her background as a systems engineer and decades of experience with NASA, Harmon prepares missions for launch and operations. This role requires careful coordination and collaboration across international partners, as the Deep Space Network’s radio antennas are spread around the world. She was responsible for ensuring the Deep Space Network was prepared to support the Artemis II spacecraft before launch.

You could not get any of that information back without the network. It’s a critical asset that also lets spacecraft know where they are. Kathleen Harmon Artemis II Mission Interface Manager for NASA's Deep Space Network

“The network has three complexes equally spaced around the world so, as the Earth rotates, one is always in view to communicate with spacecraft wherever they are in the solar system,” said Harmon.

At any given moment, the Deep Space Network complex that is currently experiencing daylight is “in control” of the entire network to ensure consistent spacecraft connectivity, an operational approach the network team calls “follow the Sun.”

While the network supports NASA’s return to the Moon, working in partnership with the Near Space Network, it will continue to maintain a close watch on NASA’s fleet of spacecraft at the Moon and beyond.

“We supported Artemis II 24 hours a day, seven days a week for the entire mission with two antennas — a prime and a backup,” Harmon said. She added that while the network was supporting Artemis II, it also communicated with robotic rovers and spacecraft throughout the solar system.

While Harmon’s work has supported missions from Juno to Voyager, her contributions to the Artemis program remind her of what first inspired her to join to NASA.

“I was a very small child when the Apollo missions happened,” said Harmon. “Apollo was my earliest memory.”
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant and inspiring role played by Kathleen Harmon in NASA's Artemis II mission, focusing on the Deep Space Network's critical function in supporting space exploration. It describes a tangible, large-scale impact on space communication infrastructure that benefits multiple missions and advances human space exploration, providing detailed context about the technology and its importance.
articles.personality_title: Kathleen Harmon leads NASA’s Deep Space Network for Artemis II mission
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Kathleen Harmon was inspired as a child by the Apollo moon missions. Today, she works at NASA managing communication for Artemis II, a mission aiming to return humans to the Moon.

**What happened** – Harmon is the mission interface manager for NASA’s Deep Space Network (DSN). This network uses giant radio antennas around the world to send and receive messages from spacecraft exploring space. She helped prepare the DSN to support Artemis II by coordinating with international teams to ensure continuous contact with the spacecraft.

**Impact** – The Deep Space Network is vital because it acts like a global GPS and phone system for spacecraft. Without it, missions like Artemis II couldn’t send back images or data from space. Harmon’s work keeps Artemis II connected 24/7 and also supports other missions to planets and beyond, making space exploration possible.

**What’s next step** – The DSN will continue supporting Artemis missions and other space exploration projects. Harmon and her team will keep improving communication to help NASA explore the Moon, Mars, and farther into space.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Kathleen Harmon plays a key role in NASA’s Artemis II mission by managing the global network that keeps spacecraft connected to Earth.
articles.personality_title_fr: Kathleen Harmon dirige le réseau de communication de la mission Artemis II de la NASA
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Kathleen Harmon a été inspirée enfant par les missions Apollo vers la Lune. Aujourd’hui, elle travaille à la NASA en gérant les communications pour Artemis II, une mission qui vise à ramener des humains sur la Lune.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Harmon est responsable de l’interface mission pour le Deep Space Network (DSN) de la NASA. Ce réseau utilise de grandes antennes radio réparties dans le monde pour envoyer et recevoir des messages des engins spatiaux. Elle a aidé à préparer le DSN pour soutenir Artemis II en coordonnant avec des équipes internationales afin d’assurer un contact continu avec le vaisseau.

**Impact** – Le Deep Space Network est essentiel car il agit comme un GPS et un téléphone mondial pour les engins spatiaux. Sans lui, les missions comme Artemis II ne pourraient pas envoyer d’images ou de données depuis l’espace. Le travail de Harmon maintient Artemis II connectée 24 heures sur 24 et soutient aussi d’autres missions vers les planètes et au-delà, rendant l’exploration spatiale possible.

**Prochaine étape** – Le DSN continuera de soutenir les missions Artemis et d’autres projets d’exploration spatiale. Harmon et son équipe amélioreront les communications pour aider la NASA à explorer la Lune, Mars et plus loin dans l’espace.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Kathleen Harmon joue un rôle clé dans la mission Artemis II de la NASA en gérant le réseau mondial qui maintient les engins spatiaux en contact avec la Terre.
articles.personality_title_es: Kathleen Harmon lidera la red de comunicación de la misión Artemis II de la NASA
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Kathleen Harmon se inspiró cuando era niña al ver las misiones Apollo a la Luna. Hoy trabaja en la NASA gestionando las comunicaciones para Artemis II, una misión que busca llevar humanos de regreso a la Luna.

**Qué pasó** – Harmon es la gerente de interfaz de misión para la Red del Espacio Profundo (Deep Space Network, DSN) de la NASA. Esta red usa grandes antenas de radio en todo el mundo para enviar y recibir mensajes de las naves espaciales. Ella ayudó a preparar la DSN para apoyar Artemis II coordinando con equipos internacionales para asegurar contacto continuo con la nave.

**Impacto** – La Red del Espacio Profundo es vital porque funciona como un GPS y teléfono global para las naves espaciales. Sin ella, misiones como Artemis II no podrían enviar imágenes o datos desde el espacio. El trabajo de Harmon mantiene a Artemis II conectada las 24 horas y también apoya otras misiones a planetas y más allá, haciendo posible la exploración espacial.

**Próximo paso** – La DSN seguirá apoyando las misiones Artemis y otros proyectos de exploración espacial. Harmon y su equipo continuarán mejorando las comunicaciones para ayudar a la NASA a explorar la Luna, Marte y más lejos en el espacio.

**Resumen en una frase** – Kathleen Harmon desempeña un papel clave en la misión Artemis II de la NASA gestionando la red global que mantiene conectadas las naves espaciales con la Tierra.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of three large, gently glowing radio antennas arranged in a circular pattern on a softly lit Earth horizon, connected by flowing lines of light symbolizing communication signals reaching out into a vast starry sky dotted with a distant crescent Moon and small spacecraft silhouettes, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones and soft blues.

articles.title: Curiosity Blog, Sols 4886-4892: Ingenuity and Perseverance, Curiosity Style
articles.summary: Written by Michelle Minitti, MAHLI Deputy Principal Investigator Earth planning date: Friday, May 8, 2026 While we know the monikers Ingenuity and Perseverance are attached to our sister helicopter and rover on the Mars 2020 mission, those characteristics were in full force with Curiosity over the past week. The science we achieved this week was […]
articles.content:
NASA’s Mars rover Curiosity acquired this image showing an oblique view into the “Atacama” drill hole, where the rover’s drill was briefly lodged. Curiosity created the image using its Mars Hand Lens Imager (MAHLI), a close-up camera located on the turret at the end of the rover's robotic arm, and an onboard focusing process that merges multiple images of the same target at different focus positions, creating a composite that brings as many features into focus as possible. Curiosity performed the focus merge on May 6, 2026 — Sol 4887, or Martian day 4,887 of the Mars Science Laboratory Mission — at 01:39:34 UTC. NASA/JPL-Caltech/MSSS

Written by Michelle Minitti, MAHLI Deputy Principal Investigator

Earth planning date: Friday, May 8, 2026

While we know the monikers Ingenuity and Perseverance are attached to our sister helicopter and rover on the Mars 2020 mission, those characteristics were in full force with Curiosity over the past week. The science we achieved this week was enabled by the ingenuity of the Curiosity engineers and scientists manifested in this extraordinary time lapse. It demonstrates the careful dance of arm motions employed — each one diligently planned by the team — to free Curiosity’s drill from the “Atacama” target. Watch the arm twist, bend, and turn with a rock slab attached, and be amazed.

The highest-priority activities after liberating the drill included imaging the drill with Mastcam and ChemCam RMI, and imaging into the now-empty drill hole with MAHLI (the image above). The science team made the most of the freshly-broken surfaces created when Atacama fell back to Mars, and the freshly-exposed sand once hidden underneath Atacama. ChemCam targeted one of the clean fracture faces with two LIBS rasters at “Tamarugal” and “Tamarugo,” and followed with another raster on a light-toned patch of bedrock formerly under Atacama at “Colchane.” MAHLI and APXS analyzed sand near Colchane at the target “Yerba Loca.” Beyond Atacama, Mastcam and ChemCam imaged the large buttes towering above our current and future drive paths. Mastcam also imaged two exposures of the polygonal fractures present in this area (targets “Cerro Elefantes” and “Azul Pampa”) and looked for wind-induced changes in the sand (“Playa los Metales”). ChemCam planned a passive spectroscopy observation of light-toned features on the “Paniri” butte and checked out a potential meteorite with a LIBS raster at "Isla Mocha.”

As engineering assessments continued, Curiosity drove uphill to study a contact between two different rock types, which can indicate a change in formation conditions, a break in time, or both. MAHLI, APXS, and ChemCam teamed up to study both rock types at the lighter-toned, layered “Toro” target and the darker, flaky “Inca de Oro” target. Mastcam planned multiple mosaics capturing different structures and transitions exposed along the contact. Across the plans during the week, REMS, RAD, and DAN regularly measured the environment above and below the rover, and Navcam and Mastcam teamed up to look for clouds, dust devils, and dust in the atmosphere.

With the health of the drill and arm confirmed by the engineers, Curiosity exhibited perseverance by heading toward a new workspace with a promising (larger) block for a new drill attempt. Our Martian exploration continues undaunted.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a successful and scientifically valuable operation by NASA's Curiosity rover on Mars, demonstrating engineering ingenuity and perseverance. The rover's ability to free its drill and continue geological studies on Mars represents a meaningful advancement in space exploration with broad public interest and scientific significance. The detailed description of the rover's activities and scientific observations provides sufficient substance and context.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s Curiosity rover frees stuck drill and continues Mars discoveries
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA’s Curiosity rover has been exploring Mars since 2012, studying rocks and the environment to learn about the planet’s past. Recently, its drill got stuck while trying to collect samples from a rock called “Atacama.”

**What happened** – Over the past week, Curiosity’s team used careful planning to move the rover’s robotic arm in a special way to free the stuck drill. After freeing it, the rover took close-up images of the drill hole and nearby rock surfaces using its cameras and tools. It then studied different rocks and sand around the area to understand Mars’ geology better.

**Impact** – This operation showed the rover’s ability to keep working even when problems arise, thanks to smart engineering and teamwork. Freeing the drill allowed scientists to examine fresh rock surfaces that had never been seen before, providing new clues about Mars’ history and environment.

**What’s next step** – With the drill working again, Curiosity will move to a new location with bigger rocks that may be better for drilling. The rover will continue its scientific mission, studying Mars’ surface and atmosphere to gather more information.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s Curiosity rover successfully freed its stuck drill and is continuing important Mars research by examining new rock targets and the planet’s environment.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le rover Curiosity de la NASA libère son foret coincé et poursuit ses découvertes sur Mars
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le rover Curiosity de la NASA explore Mars depuis 2012 pour étudier les roches et l’environnement afin de mieux comprendre l’histoire de la planète. Récemment, son foret s’est coincé en tentant de prélever un échantillon d’une roche appelée « Atacama ».

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Au cours de la dernière semaine, l’équipe de Curiosity a utilisé une planification minutieuse pour bouger le bras robotique du rover et libérer le foret coincé. Après cela, le rover a pris des images en gros plan du trou de forage et des roches environnantes avec ses caméras et instruments. Il a ensuite étudié différentes roches et du sable autour pour mieux comprendre la géologie de Mars.

**Impact** – Cette opération a montré la capacité du rover à continuer à travailler malgré les problèmes, grâce à une ingénierie intelligente et au travail d’équipe. Libérer le foret a permis aux scientifiques d’examiner des surfaces rocheuses fraîches jamais vues auparavant, fournissant de nouvelles informations sur l’histoire et l’environnement de Mars.

**Prochaine étape** – Avec un foret à nouveau fonctionnel, Curiosity se déplacera vers un nouvel endroit avec des roches plus grandes, plus adaptées au forage. Le rover poursuivra sa mission scientifique en étudiant la surface et l’atmosphère de Mars pour recueillir plus de données.

**Phrase clé** – Le rover Curiosity de la NASA a réussi à libérer son foret coincé et continue ses recherches importantes sur Mars en examinant de nouvelles roches et l’environnement de la planète.
articles.personality_title_es: El rover Curiosity de la NASA libera su taladro atascado y continúa sus descubrimientos en Marte
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El rover Curiosity de la NASA ha estado explorando Marte desde 2012 para estudiar las rocas y el ambiente y así aprender sobre el pasado del planeta. Recientemente, su taladro se quedó atascado mientras intentaba recoger muestras de una roca llamada “Atacama.”

**Qué pasó** – Durante la última semana, el equipo de Curiosity planificó cuidadosamente movimientos especiales del brazo robótico para liberar el taladro atascado. Después de liberarlo, el rover tomó imágenes de cerca del agujero de perforación y las rocas cercanas con sus cámaras y herramientas. Luego estudió diferentes rocas y arena alrededor para entender mejor la geología de Marte.

**Impacto** – Esta operación mostró la capacidad del rover para seguir trabajando incluso cuando surgen problemas, gracias a una ingeniería inteligente y trabajo en equipo. Liberar el taladro permitió a los científicos examinar superficies rocosas frescas nunca vistas antes, proporcionando nuevas pistas sobre la historia y el ambiente de Marte.

**Próximo paso** – Con el taladro funcionando de nuevo, Curiosity se moverá a un nuevo lugar con rocas más grandes que pueden ser mejores para perforar. El rover continuará su misión científica, estudiando la superficie y la atmósfera de Marte para reunir más información.

**Frase clave** – El rover Curiosity de la NASA liberó con éxito su taladro atascado y sigue realizando importantes investigaciones en Marte al examinar nuevas rocas y el ambiente del planeta.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a robotic arm gently twisting and turning with a rock slab attached, hovering over a freshly drilled, textured Martian rock surface with an intricate circular drill hole, set against a softly lit reddish-brown Mars landscape featuring distant layered buttes and polygonal fractured ground under a calm, dusty sky.
